A0:
## Recommendation

**Default to serverless functions** for a bursty API — but if you have meaningful baseline traffic or tight latency SLOs, reach for a **scale-to-zero container platform** (Cloud Run, Fargate, Knative) as the middle ground. Plain always-on containers are the wrong default for bursty workloads.

## Why serverless fits bursty traffic

The defining trait of bursty traffic is **high peak-to-average ratio** — idle or low most of the time, sharp spikes occasionally. That maps directly onto serverless's strengths:

1. **Scale-to-zero economics.** You pay per invocation/duration, not for provisioned capacity. During idle troughs the bill is ~$0. Always-on containers force you to keep a warm baseline running 24/7 — you pay for the valleys to be ready for the peaks.

2. **Near-instant elasticity.** Serverless scales concurrency per-request in seconds. Container autoscalers react slower — a new instance means image pull → boot → health check, often 30s–several minutes. To absorb a sharp spike with containers you must over-provision or pre-warm, which reintroduces the idle cost you were trying to avoid.

3. **No capacity planning.** You don't have to predict the spike size. The platform absorbs it up to your concurrency limits.

## What would flip me toward containers

- **A high, sustained baseline** under the bursts. There's a crossover point where paying per-request costs more than reserved capacity. Steady floor + occasional spikes → containers (or reserved capacity + burst overflow) win on cost.
- **Latency-sensitive bursts from cold.** Serverless **cold starts** (100ms–several seconds) are worst exactly when a spike arrives from idle. Provisioned concurrency fixes it but erodes the cost advantage.
- **Execution constraints:** long-running requests, streaming/WebSockets, large memory, GPU, or big dependency images — serverless has time/resource caps that bite here.
- **Lock-in / portability** concerns, or a complex runtime that's painful to package as a function.

## Concrete default

| Traffic shape | Pick |
|---|---|
| Spiky, low average, idle periods, no hard cold-start SLO | **Serverless functions** |
| Spiky **with** a steady baseline, or sub-100ms p99 required | **Scale-to-zero containers** (Cloud Run / Fargate) |
| Predictable high volume, long/heavy requests | **Always-on containers** + autoscaling |

If I had to commit without more detail: **start serverless.** It's the cheapest way to handle bursts, lowest ops burden, and you'll learn your real traffic curve from the bill. Migrate to scale-to-zero containers only if cold-start latency or per-request cost at the crossover point actually becomes a measured problem — don't pre-optimize for it.

The two questions that would sharpen this: **what's your baseline-to-peak ratio**, and **do you have a hard p99 latency target?** Those alone decide most of it.
A1s:
Start with serverless, and only move to containers when a specific disqualifier forces you to. Bursty traffic with quiet gaps is the textbook case where serverless wins: you scale to zero and pay nothing during the lulls, and the platform fans out to absorb spikes without you pre-provisioning capacity or guessing at peak. Containers make you choose between over-provisioning a warm pool (you pay for idle to be ready for the burst) or autoscaling that lags the spike, because new instances need to pull an image and boot — which is exactly when a sudden burst is hitting you.

The reasoning in one line: bursty load punishes capacity planning, and serverless removes the planning.

Where I'd flip to containers — if any of these are true, the serverless advantage erodes:
- **Sustained high baseline.** If "bursty" still means a high floor of constant traffic, per-request billing gets more expensive than a steadily-utilized container fleet. Serverless wins on spiky-and-idle, not spiky-on-top-of-busy.
- **Cold-start-sensitive latency.** If the burst is latency-critical (user-facing, tight SLO) and a few hundred ms to multiple seconds of cold-start tail is unacceptable, containers with a warm baseline give you consistent response times. (Provisioned concurrency on Lambda closes this gap but reintroduces idle cost.)
- **Long or heavy requests.** Execution-time limits (~15 min on Lambda), large runtimes/dependencies, GPU, or persistent connections (WebSockets, streaming) push you to containers.
- **You already run a container platform.** If there's existing Kubernetes/ECS tooling and expertise, the operational simplicity argument for serverless is weaker.

The middle option worth naming: **containerized serverless** — Cloud Run, AWS App Runner, or Fargate-on-spot-style. You package as a container (portable, no vendor-specific runtime, easy local dev) but get scale-to-zero and burst autoscaling. For a bursty API that you're nervous about locking into raw FaaS, this is often the best fit — it neutralizes the two biggest serverless objections (lock-in and packaging limits) while keeping the elasticity.

My concrete recommendation: default to **Cloud Run / App Runner (containerized serverless)** if you want portability and fewer runtime constraints, or **plain Lambda/Cloud Functions** if the workload is genuinely small, stateless, and event-driven. Reach for orchestrated containers (ECS/EKS) only when a disqualifier above applies. If you tell me your latency SLO, the rough peak-to-idle traffic ratio, and your cloud, I can make this a specific call rather than a default.
A1g:
Start with serverless, and only move to containers when a specific disqualifier forces you to. Bursty traffic with quiet gaps is the textbook case where serverless wins: you scale to zero and pay nothing during the lulls, and the platform fans out to absorb spikes without you pre-provisioning capacity or guessing at peak (AWS Lambda billing and automatic scaling, AWS Lambda Developer Guide, 2024 — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-billing.html and .../scaling.html). Containers make you choose between over-provisioning a warm pool (you pay for idle to be ready for the burst) or autoscaling that lags the spike, because new instances need to pull an image and boot — which is exactly when a sudden burst is hitting you (Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler reaction lag and image-pull latency, Kubernetes docs, 2024 — https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/).

The reasoning in one line: bursty load punishes capacity planning, and serverless removes the planning.

Where I'd flip to containers — if any of these are true, the serverless advantage erodes:
- **Sustained high baseline.** If "bursty" still means a high floor of constant traffic, per-request billing gets more expensive than a steadily-utilized container fleet. Serverless wins on spiky-and-idle, not spiky-on-top-of-busy. The crossover is workload-specific; model it against your own request volume rather than trusting a rule of thumb (cost-model comparison, "The Economics of Serverless," Bernard Golden / AWS, 2020 — https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/the-economics-of-serverless/).
- **Cold-start-sensitive latency.** If the burst is latency-critical (user-facing, tight SLO) and a cold-start tail is unacceptable, containers with a warm baseline give you consistent response times. Measured Lambda cold starts typically run from ~100ms to over 1s depending on runtime and package size (Mikhail Shilkov, "Cold Starts in AWS Lambda," 2021 — https://mikhail.io/serverless/coldstarts/aws/). Provisioned concurrency on Lambda closes this gap but reintroduces idle cost (AWS Lambda Developer Guide, provisioned concurrency, 2024 — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/provisioned-concurrency.html).
- **Long or heavy requests.** Execution-time limits (15 min hard cap on Lambda — AWS Lambda quotas, 2024 — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/gettingstarted-limits.html), large runtimes/dependencies, GPU, or persistent connections (WebSockets, streaming) push you to containers.
- **You already run a container platform.** If there's existing Kubernetes/ECS tooling and expertise, the operational simplicity argument for serverless is weaker.

The middle option worth naming: **containerized serverless** — Cloud Run, AWS App Runner, or Fargate-on-spot-style. You package as a container (portable, no vendor-specific runtime, easy local dev) but get scale-to-zero and burst autoscaling (Google Cloud Run overview, scale-to-zero and request-based autoscaling, 2024 — https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/about-instance-autoscaling). For a bursty API that you're nervous about locking into raw FaaS, this is often the best fit — it neutralizes the two biggest serverless objections (lock-in and packaging limits) while keeping the elasticity.

My concrete recommendation: default to **Cloud Run / App Runner (containerized serverless)** if you want portability and fewer runtime constraints, or **plain Lambda/Cloud Functions** if the workload is genuinely small, stateless, and event-driven. Reach for orchestrated containers (ECS/EKS) only when a disqualifier above applies.

What would change this answer: the recommendation rests on the assumption that your traffic is genuinely spiky-and-idle with a low baseline and a latency budget that can absorb an occasional cold-start tail. If you measure a high constant floor (idle time is the exception, not the rule), per-request billing loses to a steadily-utilized fleet and I'd flip to containers. If your p99 SLO is tight enough that a sub-second cold start breaks it and provisioned-concurrency idle cost rivals a warm fleet, the serverless edge disappears. And the cost crossover is the load-bearing uncertainty here — it depends on your actual peak-to-idle ratio and per-request cost, which I haven't seen, so treat the default as rebuttable by a real cost model on your numbers. If you tell me your latency SLO, the rough peak-to-idle traffic ratio, and your cloud, I can make this a specific call rather than a default.
